# byteswarm roadmap

Turns the architecture brief's `## Key features` into right-sized
`/implement-feature` chunks. Each chunk = one verifiable behavior in one
module, a PR reviewable in ~30 min. Components are the module paths inside the
byteswarm Server container (plus the CLI container).

Current state: **M1â€“M4 delivered** (+ refactors 0001â€“0003). The full path works:
webhook/CLI â†’ NATS â†’ registry â†’ Go & JS-plugin consumers â†’ SQLite/PostgreSQL
state, now with at-least-once redelivery, reconnect/durable resume, event
replay, and per-workflowID scoping. **M5 (observability) is the final
milestone** â€” make business events and errors visible to operations.

What M1â€“M4 established (reuse in M5):
- `telemetry.Emitter` port already exists: `Emit(ctx, name string, attrs
  map[string]any) error`, deny-nothing outbound seam for **consumer-defined**
  business-event sinks (ADR-0006). No adapter yet â€” that's F5.1.
- **Structured `slog` logging is already pervasive**: `main` emits JSON to
  stdout (the monitoring sink); the server middleware logs every request with a
  correlation ID; the registry logs consumer errors/panics; the bus logs
  handler failures, redelivery, reconnect. So "structured error logging" is
  largely in place â€” the one gap is an HTTP handler-panic recovery (F5.2).
- Baseline observability per design-principles: logs + `/healthz`/`/readyz`;
  OpenTelemetry only if a future ADR records the need (out of scope for M5).

---

## M1 â€” An operator publishes an event from the CLI and a built-in Go consumer handles it, end-to-end over NATS.

Foundations, minimally: the Bus backbone + dispatch + ingress + CLI contract,
proven by the lowest-risk consumer kind (compiled-in Go). Risk-forward: the
NATS JetStream adapter (F1.1) carries the most uncertainty, so it goes first.

- [x] **F1.1 NATS JetStream Bus adapter** Â· internal/bus Â· size M Â· depends on: â€”
  > /implement-feature NATS JetStream adapter for the event.Bus port.
  > - Component: internal/bus (new module; adapter implementing internal/event.Bus)
  > - Behavior: connect to NATS JetStream (URL from config/env, ADR-0004/0006); `Publish` maps an Event to subject `bw.evt.<type>.<workflowID>`; `Subscribe(subject, handle)` binds a durable pull/push consumer and invokes handle per message. Connection lifecycle owned here; domain stays transport-free. Ack/redelivery semantics deferred to M4 â€” M1 uses simple auto-ack to prove the path.
  > - Out of scope: durable-cursor recovery & explicit ack (F4.1/F4.2), replay (F4.3), workflowID scoping (F4.4), the in-memory bus.
  > - Acceptance: integration test against an embedded/test NATS server â€” publish then receive the same event; subject encoding asserted. Skips cleanly if no NATS available locally (CI provides one).

- [x] **F1.2 Consumer registry & dispatch** Â· internal/consumer Â· size M Â· depends on: F1.1
  > /implement-feature consumer registry that routes delivered events to subscribed Consumers.
  > - Component: internal/consumer (registry over the existing Consumer port)
  > - Behavior: register `Consumer`s by their `Events()`; on a bus delivery, route to every consumer subscribed to that event type; each handler runs panic-recovered and isolated (ADR-0001). A registry `Run(ctx, bus)` subscribes the needed subjects and dispatches. Global-broadcast event type routes to all consumers (F4.5 elaborates edge cases).
  > - Out of scope: the NATS wiring itself (F1.1), main composition (F1.5), script consumers (already implement the port â€” no change here), at-least-once ack (F4.1).
  > - Acceptance: unit tests with a fake Bus â€” dispatch to the right consumers by type, one consumer's panic doesn't stop the others, unknown type is a no-op.

- [x] **F1.3 Event submit HTTP endpoint** Â· internal/server Â· size S Â· depends on: F1.1
  > /implement-feature HTTP endpoint that accepts an event submission and publishes it.
  > - Component: internal/server (inbound adapter; calls event.Publisher)
  > - Behavior: `POST /events` accepts a JSON event (type, workflowID, payload), validates & bounds it at the boundary (security-fundamentals), publishes via the Publisher port, returns 202. Reuses the existing correlation-ID/logging middleware.
  > - Out of scope: webhook auth + the trigger-source endpoint (M3), CLI (F1.4), replay/audit endpoints (M4).
  > - Acceptance: handler tests with a fake Publisher â€” valid submit â†’ 202 + published; malformed/oversize â†’ 400, nothing published.

- [x] **F1.4 CLI `publish` command** Â· cmd/byteswarmctl Â· size S Â· depends on: F1.3
  > /implement-feature CLI publish subcommand that submits an event to the server.
  > - Component: cmd/byteswarmctl (consumes the F1.3 HTTP contract)
  > - Behavior: `byteswarmctl publish --type T --workflow W --payload '<json>'` (server addr from flag/env) POSTs to `/events`, reports the result/exit code. Stdlib flag package, matching the existing `version` command style.
  > - Out of scope: other operator commands, output formatting beyond pass/fail.
  > - Acceptance: test the request the command builds (against an httptest server); non-2xx â†’ non-zero exit.

- [x] **F1.5 Compose the tracer bullet + example Go consumer** Â· cmd/byteswarm Â· size S Â· depends on: F1.2, F1.3
  > /implement-feature wire the server composition root so a published event reaches a consumer.
  > - Component: cmd/byteswarm (composition root)
  > - Behavior: construct the NATS Bus (F1.1), the registry (F1.2), and the HTTP server (F1.3); register one example built-in Go consumer (logs the event it receives) to prove the chain; graceful shutdown already exists. This is the end-to-end tracer bullet.
  > - Out of scope: plugin-host wiring (M2), store wiring (M2), auth (M3).
  > - Acceptance: an end-to-end test (or scripted run) â€” `publish` a test event and observe the example consumer logging it; CI `verify` green.

---

## M2 â€” Durable state + script plugins go live: an operator declares a JavaScript plugin in config and it reacts to an event and persists namespaced state (SQLite locally, PostgreSQL in production).

Adds the `internal/store` Repository adapters behind the existing
`consumer.Repository` port, then wires the goja host (built in refactor-0001)
into the composition root so script plugins become live consumers. Risk-forward:
F2.1's pure-Go SQLite driver choice (to preserve `CGO_ENABLED=0`) is the most
uncertain piece, so it leads.

- [x] **F2.1 SQLite Repository adapter** Â· internal/store Â· size M Â· depends on: â€”
  > /implement-feature SQLite adapter for the consumer.Repository port.
  > - Component: internal/store (new module; outbound adapter implementing consumer.Repository â€” `Load(ctx, id) ([]byte, error)`, `Save(ctx, id, state []byte) error`).
  > - Behavior: on `database/sql` (ADR-0005) with a **pure-Go SQLite driver (`modernc.org/sqlite`) to keep the `CGO_ENABLED=0` static build** (ADR-0006). `New(path)` opens the DB and ensures a `consumer_state(id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, state BLOB, updated_at)` table; `Load`/`Save` use **parameterised queries only** (security-fundamentals). A missing key returns `(nil, nil)` â€” "no state yet". Include a reusable Repository **contract test** helper (exercises any consumer.Repository) so F2.2 can reuse it.
  > - Out of scope: PostgreSQL (F2.2), plugin/host wiring (F2.3), a migrations tool, per-consumer table-per-aggregate (single namespaced table is enough now â€” the plugin store shim already prefixes keys).
  > - Acceptance: integration test on a temp-file DB (`t.TempDir()`) â€” Saveâ†’Load round-trip; overwrite; missing key â†’ `(nil, nil)`; the shared contract test passes. Justify the `modernc.org/sqlite` dep in the PR (permitted tier: the ADR-0005 SQLite driver under the no-cgo invariant).

- [x] **F2.2 PostgreSQL Repository adapter (BYTEA, ADR-0009)** Â· internal/store Â· size M Â· depends on: F2.1
  > /implement-feature PostgreSQL adapter for the consumer.Repository port.
  > - Component: internal/store (second adapter behind the same port; `database/sql` + **pgx** â€” default dependency tier, ADR-0005).
  > - Behavior: `NewPostgres(dsn)` against a `consumer_state(id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, state JSONB, updated_at)` table (JSONB per ADR-0005), same `Load`/`Save` contract and missing-key semantics as F2.1; parameterised queries; DSN from env/config, never hard-coded. Reuse F2.1's Repository contract test to prove behavioral parity.
  > - Out of scope: composition-root store selection (F2.3), migrations tooling, SQLite (F2.1).
  > - Acceptance: run the shared contract test against Postgres gated on `BYTESWARM_TEST_PG_DSN` (skip cleanly if unset). NOTE: exercising this in CI needs a Postgres **service** in `verify.yml` â€” there is no pure-Go embedded Postgres, so this is a CI/infra follow-up (regenerate CI via `generate-cicd` or add the service + a checklist item); flag it in the PR rather than editing the generated pipeline by hand.

- [x] **F2.3 Wire the goja plugin host into the composition root** Â· cmd/byteswarm Â· size S Â· depends on: F2.1, F1.2, F1.5
  > /implement-feature wire internal/plugin's Host so script plugins run as live consumers.
  > - Component: cmd/byteswarm (composition root).
  > - Behavior: construct a `consumer.Repository` (SQLite from F2.1 by default, path from env), then `plugin.NewHost(repo, pub, allow, root, logger)` where `pub` is the bus (F1.5) and `allow`/`root` come from config; parse a plugin config (`plugin.Parse`), `host.LoadAll(cfg)` â†’ `[]*ScriptConsumer`, and register each with `reg.RegisterSubscriber(sc)` so script plugins dispatch alongside the example Go consumer. Fail closed: a plugin that won't load stops startup. Only active when bus + repo are configured.
  > - Out of scope: the committable config-file format (F2.4 â€” here read from env/simple source), Postgres selection UX, auth (M3).
  > - Acceptance: extract a testable wiring helper (build+register ScriptConsumers from a `plugin.Config` + `Host` + fake repo) and unit-test it with a tiny inline-`script` plugin (assert the consumer is registered for its declared events); build stays green. Live end-to-end validated manually/CI.

- [x] **F2.4 Server config file (ADR-0006) carrying plugin declarations** Â· cmd/byteswarm Â· size S Â· depends on: F2.3
  > /implement-feature introduce the committable JSON config file with env overrides.
  > - Component: cmd/byteswarm (composition root reads config).
  > - Behavior: a single JSON config file (path from `BYTESWARM_CONFIG`) providing http addr, NATS URL, store selection+DSN/path, plugins dir, the exec allowlist, and the `plugins` declarations â€” with **environment variables overriding** file values (ADR-0006: file is the committable base, env overrides per-deployment/secrets). Consolidates the scattered `BYTESWARM_*` env reads; secrets stay in env, never the committed file (security-fundamentals). Uses stdlib `encoding/json` (no new dep unless a friendlier format is later chosen).
  > - Out of scope: hot-reload, schema-versioning, non-JSON formats, auth config (M3).
  > - Acceptance: unit-test config loading/merge â€” file values parsed; env overrides win; malformed config fails closed; plugin declarations flow into F2.3's host wiring. `verifier` green.

## M3 â€” External systems trigger events securely over the webhook.

- [x] **F3.1 Shared-secret authenticator** Â· internal/auth Â· size S Â· depends on: â€”
  > /implement-feature shared-secret adapter for the auth.Authenticator port.
  > - Component: internal/auth (adapter implementing the existing `Authenticator` port: `Authenticate(ctx, credential string) error`).
  > - Behavior: `NewSharedSecret(secret string) *SharedSecret` (or returning `auth.Authenticator`). `Authenticate` compares the presented credential to the configured secret using a **constant-time comparison** (`crypto/subtle.ConstantTimeCompare`) and returns `auth.ErrUnauthenticated` on any mismatch, empty credential, OR empty configured secret â€” deny by default, fail closed (reference/security-fundamentals.md). Never log the secret or the credential. The secret arrives from config/env at the composition root, never hard-coded.
  > - Out of scope: the HTTP endpoint/middleware (F3.2), stronger mechanisms (JWT/mTLS â€” the port stays swappable per ADR-0002), rotating secrets.
  > - Acceptance: table-driven unit tests â€” correct secret â†’ nil; wrong/empty credential â†’ `ErrUnauthenticated`; a `SharedSecret` built with an empty secret denies everything; assert constant-time path is used (compare via subtle). No assertion libs.

- [x] **F3.2 Authenticated webhook ingress endpoint** Â· internal/server Â· size M Â· depends on: F3.1
  > /implement-feature POST /webhook that authenticates the external trigger source, then publishes.
  > - Component: internal/server (a SECOND inbound route beside `POST /events`; consumes the `auth.Authenticator` and `event.Publisher` ports).
  > - Behavior: add `POST /webhook` that extracts a shared-secret credential from the request (e.g. `Authorization: Bearer <secret>` or a header like `X-Webhook-Secret` â€” pick one, document it), calls `Authenticator.Authenticate`, and on any error returns **401 and publishes nothing** (deny by default); on success it validates/bounds the JSON body exactly like `POST /events` (reuse that boundary logic) and publishes via `event.Publisher`, returning 202. Inject the `Authenticator` into `server.New` (extend the constructor); wire the shared-secret authenticator from `loadConfig` (secret via env, e.g. `BYTESWARM_WEBHOOK_SECRET`, per ADR-0006/security-fundamentals) in `cmd/byteswarm`. Log auth failures as security events **without** the credential or payload. Reuse the correlation-ID/logging middleware.
  > - Out of scope: per-source secrets / multiple credentials, replay protection & HMAC-signed bodies (future), the CLI path (`POST /events` stays as-is), auth on `/events` (operator-local, unchanged).
  > - Acceptance: handler tests (httptest) with a fake Authenticator + fake Publisher â€” valid secret + valid body â†’ 202 and published; missing/invalid secret â†’ 401 and **nothing published**; malformed/oversize body (authenticated) â†’ 4xx and nothing published. Assert no credential/secret appears in logs. A `cmd/byteswarm` wiring check that the webhook authenticator is built from config when the secret is set.

## M4 â€” Resilience & audit: a workflow survives consumer failures and reboots (at-least-once + reconnect), its history is replayable, and instances can scope to a workflowID.

Components are re-assigned from the original `internal/workflow` stub to where
the code actually lives (`bus`/`consumer`) â€” see the drift note up top.
Risk-forward: F4.1 (making processing failures actually redeliver) is the core
correctness gap and leads; F4.4 may expose a subject-scheme constraint (below).

- [x] **F4.1 At-least-once processing (redeliver on consumer failure)** Â· internal/consumer Â· size M Â· depends on: â€” (F1.2 done)
  > /implement-feature make the registry redeliver events whose processing failed.
  > - Component: internal/consumer (the `Registry.Run` delivery handler; the bus already Naks when the handler returns an error).
  > - Behavior: `dispatch` currently swallows consumer errors and `Run`'s handler always returns nil (always acks). Change dispatch to report whether any subscribed consumer failed, and have `Run`'s handler **return an error when processing failed** so the bus `Nak`s â†’ JetStream redelivers. Consumers must be idempotent (already a stated non-functional in the brief) since a redelivered event re-runs the type's consumers. Keep panic isolation. Add a bounded redelivery guard so a permanently-failing event doesn't loop forever (JetStream `MaxDeliver` + a logged drop, or defer a dead-letter to its own chunk â€” decide in the PR, keep it small).
  > - Out of scope: reconnect (F4.2), replay (F4.3), scoping (F4.4), a full dead-letter subsystem.
  > - Acceptance: registry unit tests with a fake Bus whose handler return is observable â€” all consumers succeed â†’ handler returns nil (ack); a consumer error â†’ handler returns non-nil (Nak/redeliver); a panic is isolated and counts as failure. If a MaxDeliver guard is added, test the give-up path.

- [x] **F4.2 Reconnect & recovery** Â· internal/bus Â· size M Â· depends on: F4.1
  > /implement-feature survive NATS disconnects and process restarts without losing position.
  > - Component: internal/bus.
  > - Behavior: configure nats.go reconnection (`nats.RetryOnFailedConnect`, max-reconnects/backoff, reconnect/disconnect handler logs) in `bus.New`; confirm the **durable** consumer (already used) resumes from its stored cursor after a reconnect or restart so unacked events are redelivered (pairs with F4.1). Surface connection state via logs.
  > - Out of scope: replay-from-arbitrary-point (F4.3), multi-cluster.
  > - Acceptance: integration test with the embedded NATS server â€” publish, re-`Subscribe` with the same durable, and confirm an unacked event is redelivered; a reconnect after a blip does not drop the durable. (Embedded-server-gated like the existing bus test.)

- [x] **F4.3 Event replay / audit read** Â· internal/bus Â· size M Â· depends on: F4.1
  > /implement-feature read historical events back from the stream for audit/replay.
  > - Component: internal/bus (a replay read primitive; a CLI surface can be a follow-up).
  > - Behavior: add `Replay(ctx, from, handle)` creating an ephemeral JetStream consumer with `DeliverByStartTime`/`DeliverByStartSequence` that streams historical events to `handle` without disturbing live durable consumers. Bounded, read-only (no republish).
  > - Out of scope: a CLI `replay` command (separate small chunk), retention changes, workflowID broker-side filtering (needs F4.4's scheme).
  > - Acceptance: embedded-NATS integration test â€” publish N events, replay from the start, receive all N in order; replay does not ack/perturb the live subscription.

- [x] **F4.4 workflowID subscription scoping** Â· internal/bus Â· size S Â· depends on: â€” (unblocked by refactor-0003)
  > /implement-feature let an instance subscribe to a single workflowID or to any.
  > - Component: internal/bus (a scoped subscription option; the registry passes the scope through).
  > - Behavior: now that `<type>` is a single token (refactor-0003 / ADR-0010), add a scope so `Subscribe` (or a new scoped variant / `bus.Config` field) targets **`bw.evt.*.<workflowID>`** for a specific workflowID, or **`event.SubjectAll`** (`bw.evt.>`) for any â€” per the brief's "each instance subscribes to a single workflowID or to any". The scope comes from config (e.g. `BYTESWARM_WORKFLOW_ID`, empty = any) and is wired at the composition root; the registry uses the chosen subject instead of always subscribing to `SubjectAll`. Broker-side filtering (not in-process), so a scoped instance only receives its workflow's events.
  > - Out of scope: at-least-once (F4.1), reconnect (F4.2), replay (F4.3); multi-workflow-set scoping (one or any, per the brief â€” not arbitrary subsets); constraining the workflowID charset (ADR-0010 left it flexible; a dotted workflowID still works as the trailing tokens for a *specific* subscription).
  > - Acceptance: bus unit/integration test (embedded NATS) â€” an instance scoped to `wfA` receives an event published with `wfA` and does **not** receive one published with `wfB`; an any-scope instance receives both. Assert the subject built for a given scope (`bw.evt.*.wfA` vs `bw.evt.>`).

- [x] **F4.5 Global broadcast event type** Â· internal/consumer Â· size S Â· depends on: F1.2
  > **Already delivered by F1.2** (not a separate PR): `consumer.BroadcastType` exists and `dispatch` routes a broadcast-typed event to every registered consumer. Ticked to reflect reality; any edge-case hardening (broadcast Ă— workflowID scoping) rides F4.4.

## M5 â€” Observability: consumers emit business events to a sink, and handler panics are captured as structured error records â€” the system is operable in production.

The last milestone. Structured logging already exists everywhere (see state
note), so M5 fills the two gaps: a concrete adapter behind the `Emitter` port,
and HTTP handler-panic recovery. Both are independent (parallelizable).

- [x] **F5.1 Business-event emitter adapter** Â· internal/telemetry Â· size M Â· depends on: â€” (F1.1's bus is unrelated â€” emission is outbound to sinks, not the bus)
  > /implement-feature slog-backed adapter for the telemetry.Emitter port.
  > - Component: internal/telemetry (adapter implementing the existing `Emitter` port: `Emit(ctx, name string, attrs map[string]any) error`).
  > - Behavior: a default emitter that routes a business event to a **sink**, with structured logging as the baseline sink (design-principles: logs first, OTel only on a future ADR). `NewEmitter(sink)` where the default sink is an `*slog.Logger`: `Emit` writes one structured record â€” the event `name`, the `attrs`, and the correlation ID from ctx (reuse the same correlation key the server sets) â€” at info level to a dedicated business-event logger. Keep the sink a small interface (or just `*slog.Logger`) so a real external/HTTP sink (consumer-defined, ADR-0006) can replace it later without changing callers. Do not log secrets â€” document that `attrs` are caller-controlled and must not carry credentials/PII (security-fundamentals). Validate a non-empty `name`.
  > - Out of scope: OpenTelemetry (needs a future ADR), concrete external HTTP sinks, per-consumer sink routing, and wiring `Emit` into Go consumers / the plugin capability API (a follow-up â€” the port exists, this gives it an adapter).
  > - Acceptance: unit tests â€” `Emit` writes a record containing the event name + attrs (capture via an `slog` handler over a `bytes.Buffer`, or a fake sink); the correlation ID from ctx appears when present; empty name â†’ error; a sink error propagates. Table-driven, stdlib testing, no assertion libs.

- [ ] **F5.2 HTTP panic-recovery + structured error logging** Â· internal/server Â· size S Â· depends on: â€”
  > /implement-feature recovery middleware that turns a handler panic into a logged 500.
  > - Component: internal/server (a `func(http.Handler) http.Handler` middleware in the existing chain, before the mux).
  > - Behavior: recover panics from any HTTP handler, log a **structured error record** (level error) with the correlation ID, method, and path â€” but **never** the request body, panic value details that could leak secrets, or a stack in the response â€” and write a generic `500` (reuse `writeError`). Add it to the `New` middleware chain (outermost so it wraps everything, alongside correlationID + requestLogger). This completes the "structured error/exception logging" story for the ingress; the rest is already covered by existing slog logging across bus/consumer/main. Re-homed from the original `internal/telemetry` stub to `internal/server`, where HTTP error handling lives.
  > - Out of scope: recovery for non-HTTP goroutines (consumers already recover panics in the registry; the bus callback is guarded), changing the log format/sink.
  > - Acceptance: handler test â€” a route that panics returns 500 (not a dropped connection), the server keeps serving subsequent requests, and a structured error line is logged carrying the correlation ID but not the panic payload. stdlib `httptest`, no assertion libs.

### Deferred follow-ups (not milestones â€” pick up as needed)
- **Expose `Emit` to consumers/plugins**: wire the F5.1 emitter into Go consumers and add an `emit` capability to the plugin host API (internal/plugin) so plugins can emit business telemetry.
- **CLI `replay` command**: an operator-facing wrapper over `bus.Replay` (F4.3).
- **Dead-letter handling**: terminal handling/advisory for events that exhaust `MaxDeliver` (F4.1 bounds them; a DLQ/advisory sink is the next step).
